text: Hi, this is Dr. Augustino Cervone. I produced this video for patient education to help explain what it's like to perform a lysis of adhesions. Here I'm using the robot and setting up the instrumentations in order to perform this. This is a patient who has had previous surgery and now has an abdominal hernia. we'll be repairing the hernia.

The camera is introduced into the abdomen, revealing normal intestinal contents. The anterior abdominal wall shows scarring from previous surgery, with the omentum suspended. The surgeon begins to navigate through the scar tissue.

The surgeon uses fine scissors and occasional cautery to dissect through adhesions, freeing the anterior abdominal wall. The robotic system provides high-definition, three-dimensional imagery, allowing precise manipulation of tissue despite the lack of tactile feedback.

The surgeon releases adhesions using cautery, carefully navigating through fat tissue. As the dissection approaches the intestines, the surgeon proceeds with caution to avoid injury, highlighting the importance of slow and deliberate movements.

Now as much as this can be done through an open incision, as well as done laparoscopically, performing this robotically, we tend to have a steadier hand and are able to visualize almost individual strands of the scar tissue. As you can see, this becomes a tedious process. It requires patience and certainly the skill in developing these tissue planes. I will also say that as much as we expect there to be adhesions in a patient who has had previous operations, certainly somebody with an abdominal wall hernia, we would expect adhesions. We can never tell without actually looking in there what's involved, what's going to be involved, how much time is needed, and what's to be expected. No matter if somebody has had CAT scans, ultrasounds, other studies that didn't involve us being in the abdomen, it's impossible to tell beforehand. And as you can see, this is a process that can take a period of time.

And certainly, it can take longer than the actual repair of the hernia. And from a surgeon's perspective, the longer time we're in the operating room performing any portion of an operation, the more fatigue that we encounter. As you saw at the beginning of this video, in performing robotic surgery, the surgeon sits behind a console. This limits the amount of fatigue. I would also add that comfort of the surgeon potentially translates into better outcomes for the patient. And my reason for saying that is that when performing robotic surgery, the surgeon is relatively comfortable behind the console. And taking it to a sports analogy, the surgeon gets into his or her zone, so to speak, when performing robotics.
Well, I hope you've enjoyed my little video on license of adhesions, and hopefully it's created some clarity.
